word.item: I will go along with you
I go along the bank following the current
I am not going with you
A rope crosses the bayou (for a ferryboat)
How did you (s.) go there; Read: he
it is necessary for me to leave
Where did you (f. pl) go yesterday?
Where did you (m. pl) go yesterday?
I will be there (to do that)
they go hunting to kill deer
we 2 are going to paint each other
I (cause him to?) have kicked him
I have begun to kick, I want to kick away
he is trying to kick him
sometimes (G), no, its not that way (V.)
shallow water of river, sand bar
Henry would have been drowned in the water had he not known how to swim
one third, "beyond (i.e. less than) one half"
a thicket of bushes (lit. young trees)
the end of the moon, "the moon finished", when its invisible
("Charnaie" the earliest grain (seed?) producing tree, 15-20 growing in bunches in the water) March
An old person (woman) lived in a corner of the village
when he brought there was a fine house there
I would go to town if I had money
she would go to town if she had money
a lazy man, a man good for nothing
harvest, "when you want to plant your crop" (tapa, vegetables, what is planted)
brains "what is enclosed in the head"
I begin to measure my ground
they cause (a sick person) to sweat
they cause him to be purged
they cause it to be mixt
the bottom of a lake (bottle, etc.)
I remove the cover and throw it away
cloud, clouds, heaven, sky, clear sky
poor me! I am lying down
I am lying flat on my belly (f. hepi)
almost (a doubtful form) (tu is perhaps the dim. suff.; Volsine gave almost as ho´tit-ak, but he evidently confused "almost" with "most all")
the red-shouldered chicken-hawk; (Cr. mangeur des poules)
just, just so far, just that much (probably related to tiric q.v.)
my wrist "just the point of my hand"
just so far and no farther
at the end of this time
in a little while, before long
quick (the quick movement one makes when scared)
the red-headed woodpecker (?), Creole oisean peru
pokeweed, also given as a cactus that yields water; (Cr. chougras)
to fold, to bend, a bend
if he had not known how to swim
the thunder is recognized as he
to put around, encircle, the neck (?)
I put around the neck, or over the shoulders
silver buttons worn on costumes of Indians in later times
silver pieces like little bells worn on the clothing
a necklace composed of silver pieces (V. and J.)
what? (said when one has not understood a question)
above (on the arm), above in a tree (V.)
the grass is tall in some places
this tree is not as high as the house
the breath of a snake (given by Gatch- as "the hiss of a snake", probably erroneously)
one clears by cutting (the tall grass)
to cut in a wedge shape as with an ax
dumplings ("round things made out of corn")
I open the door, or passageway
Unkown to Sam; Point Coupie City, "place above the Mississippi" (i.e. on a bluff)
to paint, make a figure or picture of
to put at the base of
when it (f.) has gone up and disappeared
he finishes speaking to them in a discourse
a keeper of a fast (the keeper of the cemeteries is called ta´waka, there is one under him called huma´ra tahă´ra; the latter has to report to him every day)
I begin to sing a song
I stand with legs spread apart
said of things shining; the sight, image
to mix, to put to together
space between fingers (for he´kunîc also he´kun sin)
this boy is able to climb all trees
to crawl, move (as snakes, clouds, etc.)
my uncle wants me to remain here (G.) (Johnson could not understand this and thought it was incorrect)
sister and fem. cousin of m., brother of f.
What person has told you that?
that is why I am finding myself so sad
on that side, in that direction
where I was reared, not long ago I was a young man
I want to play or have a good time (J.); (V. could not understand it)
name for a town in Louisiana, given by V. as "Mississippi"
a mole with a pointed nose, shrew (?), "round (nosed) mouse"
I am pushing him into that (G.); I amm planing (V.)
(f. with ending -tc, or m. with -ku), dew
come on (V.), let us walk along
design to ornament a house, pretty marks put on
dance accompanied by song (as in old times)
dance of a the wood-lice, both sexes
dance of the chickens, 1 man and women together; 4 different songs
dance of the horses; 2 sexes cross in files
dance of the ducks; each line is one sex, and the lines approach each other
dance of the fox; they dance behind the men;
dance of the chickens of the Alibamu
dance of the pumpkins (juromont, Creole word; Du Pratz giramont)
dance of the alligator; 2 sexes hold each other's hand in a circle;
dance of the commencement of midnight; a man leads the dance;
prickly ash, "tree which is medicine for the teeth"
"dead man's remedy", a small low, red flower, 2-3 in. tall growing in wet places of the woods, etc.
peppermint, "big white medicine herb" (Johnson said there are 2 kinds, but he did not know which was referred to.) Poss. sheepweed, l'arbre mouton
What is the name of the man who is passing?
I remove the cover (taking it away entirely)
I am boring a hole with a gimlet
I am dividing, I am partaking
I descend the hill or slope
we get off of the train
do you they that the fire is extinct?
they are all finished, i.e. dead
the son of my father's sister
common robin, also given as the little blue heron; by Gat. as the dove, and by Johnson as the "snowbird"; the Cr. name is petit gris. Wren.
fasting, chaste, abstaining (during fasts a man ate only one meal a day, and this not before noon, and he did not even so much as speak to a woman)
the sail (or flag) flaps in the wind
petticoat, robe; to put on a petticoat, robe
I am pushing away or aside (from someone)
he is to wear fine clothing
I fold this web; a loom
the flag flaps in the wind
cicada (cr. la cigale), also given a katydid and locust
if the harvest of the plantation is good things will be cheap
if I come, I will come in the morning
I am obliged to aid them (G), he came and helped me (J)
From which bank of the river?
If I am not cured there is no payment
they are going to pay me
a floor to walk on (V.) steps (J.)
latch with cord ("for nothing" because it has no real key)
to set something straight up, to stand erect
I stand with legs spread asunder
the stones are standing on opp. banks
I bend some things, it leans on one side
I will set it forth ( a speech or description )
to make a clucking noise, glu, glu
sing glu, glu ( like a turkey )
cross, 2) to walk, ie. to cross the legs, to step
I cross and put it down
the cross planted, erected (G). standing with one leg behind the other (V.)
on this side of the stone
I am lying on one side
I stop (J.), where are you going?
I have the habit of pinching
haha! bravo! good! well! (exclamation of satisfaction)
a dish, pot, piece of earthenware, ceramic
I have the habit of scratching
a sun halo or moon halo
I have the habit of scratching
(m.) round, a circle; (f.) round, a circle
crown of the head; also scalp (?)
to assemble (refers to a custom of assembling to dance around the dead)
stormy weather, "a multitude or an assembly of rainstorms"
a polypod which grows on the bark of green wood, "hunting medicine"
"bird looking upward", sun-gazer, or Am. bittern; Cr. vise en l'air
"water pond bird", the tame duck (V. & J.); Gat. called this the "wagtail" prob. erroneously
the fish swims with its fins
they 2 finished putting feathers on their arrows
I crush many times (as in the case of brains which it is desire to make penetrate skins thoroughly when tanning them)
my brother's son, my nephex, "my other son"
my stepson (G.), cousin (son of father's brother) (J.)
past (pf.) I have been chief (and am no longer)
the night will never come, it will never be night
daubed, spotted, large placards or spots on mats, animals, etc.

interlinear-text.item: Includes a Choctaw and Mobilian word
On 4th line, cross-reference to another notebook, but notebook number is unclear. Possibly notebook 4?

word.item: see XV 119a, another variety of silver button
minute silver pieces worn as ornaments on clothing
woodcock, also given as bustard and wild goose (Creole outarde)
if the doctor treating me does not cure there is no payment
said to be a small variety of beaver
to hoe, dig with the mattock, cultivate
to stick out, pull back, move (said of the tongue only)
I am pulling the tongue out
to enclose; by der. stopper and button
something (such as a sore) burst on me
a discourd, to discourse, to explain
I go in a straight line
it is moist or foggy weather
"the soul burner"; i.e. the sorceress, the witch
who resuscitates, always survives, indestructible (applies to persons)
holes in their noses, in their beaks (f.)
(m.) a little hole full of water, water-hole
perforation in the ear lobe, etc.
I am stirring up something (flour, etc) with water or other liquid
I keep the history, the relation
(said when a dog is lapping something in a pot)
it is good that he is dead, like he was dead
a dead horse, swollen up, lying down (poison or in the sun)
suff. forming infinitives and participles, also independent personal pronouns; often used as the equivalent of the conj."and" , "and then"
a person who has nothing to do
the one of the two, the other, the one , each
on the other (bank of the river)
I make a hole thru a stone
to punish, give one bad luck,make him unlucky, do something he will not like, to hoodoo
it will not be good for him (v), something he does not like (j), punishment (g)
to love, dear (an endearing term); to be dear, expensive
to gather, to pick (as cotton), to break a piece off (v) a piece
to change, to learn by oneself, to change oneself into an animal (g), to accomplish or obtain supernatural power
I shake (i.e. change position of) the head
sorceress, witch, "woman who does evil", a prostitute
the nonpareil or red-eyed vireo, creole ti gris
what I put around (V & J), i circle or go around something (G)
a spool of thread rolling of itself; Gatrch calls me´rku a "bobbin"
child! (in direct address), there was a chief of this name
down there, far (more than a mile)
down there (said of a village, etc.)
on the other side of the stone
the water is heavier than the oil
the man below is still visible
to cast a reflection, brightness (of the sun only)
to cast a relection on someone, lightening (v)
he lighted her with the reflection
I am sweeping with a broom
mattress filled with moss, a cushion (V.)
a steamboat captain, "little chief of a fire-smoke boat"
a little mound, an elevation--sometimes as high as a house
seems to be a sign of the dual altho occurring in the plural of all obj. pronominal forms except one because with that exception the objective forms do not differentiate the dual and plural)
war, the war in a city, "d'un pays la guerre"
is the French name [of the Natchez], says Sam
(m.) dogwood, arrow-wood; Creole, bois de flèche (somewhat uncertain)
convex or protuberant (like breast of a woman)
some kind of rock sticking together, large stone having numerous round places on the surface
(m.) shore, bank, hillock, bluff, cliff, mountain
wooden roller; beam, a rolling log (V.)
to remain with or live with
he had remained with him, you stayed with him (V.)
a bug that lives in rotten wood; about 1½ inches long
going to dance (J.); a dance accompanied by song as in older time (G.)
new, young, just, at present, etc.
a child that has just been born, or is just being born
a horned owl, 6-7 in. long with a big head & feather ears 3 in. long, it flies at night
excl. when one forgets something, also equivalent to English oh!
(given by Gatschet as "green", but perhaps it was intended for o´xcta me´li(n))
sorceress, one who poisons, witch; shaman
sorcerer, lit. "who changes himself into an animal"; name of Baptiste Cassetête & his wife
the caul, fat film enveloping the breast of the hog, leaf lard
a branch, limb; poss. also brush, brushes
no, none at all, not (sometimes pa + ha appear to be separated)
to be dressed up finely epsecially about the head
to be dressed up finely epsecially about the head
a hat with a peacock's tail on it
I plaster it on him (his head?)
we put it on his head, or we put a plaster on him
split, to split (along the grain of the wood) into thin boards, to rive, to cleave
minute silver pieces worn as ornaments on clothing
happiness, also I had a lucky day (betting, etc.)
